map.condition: Need to show f(a)=f(b) implies f'(c)=0 for some c in (a,b)
Need the average rate of change equals instantaneous rate at some point
Two functions, need ratio of differences equals ratio of derivatives
Bounding error or proving monotonicity on an interval

formulas.name: Rolle's Theorem — Conditions
formulas.latex: f \text{ continuous on } [a,b],\ f \text{ differentiable on } (a,b),\ f(a)=f(b)
formulas.plain: f continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and f(a) = f(b)
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying that all three hypotheses hold before applying Rolle's conclusion
formulas.trap: Differentiability is required on the OPEN interval (a,b); continuity is required on the CLOSED interval [a,b]. Missing one condition invalidates the theorem.

formulas.name: Rolle's Theorem — Conclusion
formulas.plain: There exists c in (a,b) such that f'(c) = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Asserting existence of a critical point when endpoint values are equal

terms.means: the point where the tangent is horizontal

formulas.trap: Rolle's theorem guarantees existence but not uniqueness of c. There may be multiple such points.

formulas.name: Rolle's Theorem — Geometric Interpretation
formulas.plain: The tangent line at c is horizontal (slope = 0)
formulas.whenToUse: Visualizing why Rolle's theorem holds — curve must turn around if endpoints are equal

terms.means: point where function achieves local max or min in (a,b)

formulas.trap: A function can satisfy f(a)=f(b) and still be non-differentiable at the turning point (e.g., |x|), so Rolle's theorem would not apply.

formulas.name: Lagrange MVT — Conditions
formulas.latex: f \text{ continuous on } [a,b] \text{ and differentiable on } (a,b)
formulas.plain: f continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b)
formulas.whenToUse: Checking prerequisites before applying the MVT conclusion
formulas.trap: Polynomials always satisfy both conditions. Rational functions may fail at poles — verify domain.

formulas.name: Lagrange MVT — Core Formula
formulas.plain: f'(c) = [f(b) - f(a)] / (b - a) for some c in (a,b)
formulas.whenToUse: Relating the average rate of change over [a,b] to the instantaneous rate at some interior point

terms.means: average rate of change (slope of secant)

formulas.trap: MVT tells you c EXISTS, not its exact value. GATE questions often ask you to FIND c — solve f'(x) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) algebraically.

formulas.name: Lagrange MVT — Geometric Meaning
formulas.latex: \text{Slope of secant } PQ = \text{slope of tangent at } c
formulas.plain: Slope of secant line PQ equals slope of tangent at some c between a and b
formulas.whenToUse: Interpreting MVT geometrically in problems that describe chords and tangents
formulas.trap: The tangent at c is parallel to (not the same as) the secant PQ.

formulas.name: Rolle's Theorem as Special Case of MVT
formulas.plain: When f(a)=f(b), MVT gives f'(c)=0, recovering Rolle's Theorem
formulas.whenToUse: Justifying that Rolle's theorem is a special case and interchanging between the two
formulas.trap: Rolle's theorem requires f(a)=f(b); Lagrange MVT does not. Don't apply Rolle's when endpoint values differ.

formulas.name: Cauchy's Generalized MVT
formulas.plain: [f(b)-f(a)] / [g(b)-g(a)] = f'(c) / g'(c) for some c in (a,b)
formulas.whenToUse: Problems involving two functions on the same interval, or deriving L'Hopital's rule
formulas.trap: g(b)=g(a) or g'(c)=0 violates the theorem conditions — check that g is not constant on the interval.

formulas.name: Lagrange MVT as Special Case of Cauchy
formulas.plain: Setting g(x)=x in Cauchy MVT recovers Lagrange MVT
formulas.whenToUse: Theoretical questions asking the hierarchy: Cauchy generalizes Lagrange which generalizes Rolle
formulas.trap: The chain is Cauchy ⊃ Lagrange ⊃ Rolle — Rolle is the most restrictive (f(a)=f(b) required).

formulas.name: L'Hopital's Rule (0/0 or ∞/∞ form)
formulas.plain: lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x) when the limit is 0/0 or inf/inf
formulas.whenToUse: Evaluating indeterminate limit forms by differentiating numerator and denominator separately

terms.means: both differentiable near a, g'(x) ≠ 0 near a

formulas.trap: L'Hopital applies to 0/0 or ∞/∞ ONLY. For 0·∞, 1^∞, 0^0, 0^∞ you must first rewrite into 0/0 or ∞/∞ form.

formulas.name: Indeterminate Forms — Reduction to 0/0
formulas.plain: 0*inf = f/(1/g); 1^inf, 0^0, inf^0 => e^(lim g*ln f) then apply L'Hopital
formulas.whenToUse: Rewriting product/exponential indeterminate forms before applying L'Hopital
formulas.trap: Forgetting to exponentiate back after taking the logarithm — the limit you compute with L'Hopital is the exponent, not the original limit.

formulas.name: Monotonicity via MVT
formulas.latex: f'(x) > 0 \;\forall x \in (a,b) \Rightarrow f \text{ strictly increasing on } (a,b)
formulas.plain: f'(x) > 0 for all x in (a,b) => f is strictly increasing on (a,b)
formulas.whenToUse: Proving or checking whether a function is monotone on an interval
formulas.trap: f'(c)=0 at isolated points does not break strict monotonicity (e.g., x^3 at x=0).

formulas.name: Constant Function Corollary
formulas.plain: f'(x) = 0 everywhere on (a,b) implies f is constant
formulas.whenToUse: Proving two functions are equal if their difference has zero derivative everywhere
formulas.trap: Valid only on a CONNECTED interval. On a disconnected domain, different constants can appear on each component.

formulas.name: Lipschitz / Error Bound via MVT
formulas.whenToUse: Bounding the change in f when the derivative is bounded, or proving Lipschitz continuity

terms.means: upper bound on |f'| over the interval

formulas.trap: This bound is tight only when |f'| is constant. If f' varies, M may overestimate the actual change.

formulas.name: At Most One Root (Strict Monotonicity)
formulas.latex: f'(x) \neq 0 \;\forall x \in (a,b) \Rightarrow f \text{ has at most one root in } (a,b)
formulas.plain: If f'(x) != 0 everywhere on (a,b), then f has at most one root in (a,b)
formulas.whenToUse: Proving uniqueness of a solution or root in a given interval
formulas.trap: MVT-based uniqueness requires f' to be NOWHERE zero on (a,b). A single zero of f' destroys the guarantee.

formulas.name: MVT-Based Inequality Proof Template
formulas.latex: f(b) - f(a) = f'(c)(b-a) \Rightarrow m(b-a) \leq f(b)-f(a) \leq M(b-a)
formulas.plain: m*(b-a) <= f(b)-f(a) <= M*(b-a) where m=min f', M=max f' on [a,b]
formulas.whenToUse: Deriving two-sided bounds on f(b)-f(a) from min/max of f' — common GATE proof technique
formulas.trap: Requires f' to be bounded on [a,b]; applies only when the interval is closed and bounded.

formulas.name: Taylor's Theorem (MVT generalization)
formulas.whenToUse: Approximating functions and bounding truncation error; MVT is the n=1 case

terms.means: some point between a and x (Lagrange remainder)

formulas.trap: When n=1, Taylor's theorem reduces exactly to Lagrange's MVT: f(b)=f(a)+f'(c)(b-a).

formulas.name: MVT as Taylor n=1 (First-Order)
formulas.plain: f(b) = f(a) + f'(c)*(b-a) for some c between a and b
formulas.whenToUse: Rewriting MVT in additive form to directly estimate f(b) given f(a) and derivative bounds

terms.means: linear correction term, akin to Taylor remainder of order 1

formulas.trap: This form is exact (not an approximation) — c is guaranteed to exist, so the equality holds, not just approximately.
